Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after work, but being 27 and having bills to pay meant I couldn't always stick to a schedule. I'd usually listen to recorded lectures while commuting or doing chores since I learn better by hearing things anyway. Some weeks I'd manage 17 hours of studying, other weeks barely anything because life just got in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life outside university. I work part-time and that schedule changes week to week, so I couldn't really establish consistent study times. When deadlines approached, I'd cram more, but otherwise I'd fit studying into whatever gaps I could find. It wasn't ideal, but with limited resources and no internet at home, I had to be flexible.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I was on campus because that's the only place I have reliable internet access. I'd check for new announcements, download materials to study later at home, or see if any assignments were due soon. Sometimes a classmate would mention something was posted and that would prompt me to go check it out during my next campus visit.
Q4: Honestly, using the LMS never really became a smooth routine for me. Without internet at home, I had to plan my LMS use around being physically at university, which I couldn't do as often as I needed. I'd try to batch my online activities—downloading lectures, checking discussion boards, submitting what I could—all in one session. It felt more like a chore I had to schedule rather than something natural.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful to me because I could download them and listen repeatedly at home. I'm an auditory learner, so being able to replay explanations helped concepts stick better than reading textbooks. The discussion posts were also helpful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of—it was like getting extra perspectives on confusing topics.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs because I just didn't have time to sit and read through everything. With my learning style, reading dense text doesn't stick as well anyway. I'd prioritize anything I could listen to over things I had to read, which probably hurt my assignment completion rate but felt necessary given my constraints.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on the exam or in assignments. I also valued anything I could engage with through listening rather than reading. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade, I'd often skip it because I simply didn't have hours to spare on things that seemed optional.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're juggling work, limited resources, and trying to pass a course, optional stuff falls to the bottom of the list. Required activities at least had clear stakes attached to them. I knew I should do more optional work to deepen my understanding, but survival mode meant focusing only on what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while cooking dinner. I took handwritten notes from those audio files and reviewed them at home where I had no internet. My assignment scores were actually quite good because I put real effort into the work itself, even if my online presence looked sparse.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures, rewinding parts I didn't understand, making audio notes to myself on my phone. I'd discuss concepts with classmates in person or over text. The LMS only saw me when I physically came to campus and logged in, which wasn't often enough.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once on campus, download everything I could, and then study from those files for days or weeks without logging back in. One lecture video might get watched three or four times at home, but the system only recorded that single download. My real engagement was invisible to the platform.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. Work picked up extra shifts I couldn't refuse because I needed the money, and I started feeling this heavy sadness about falling behind. My attendance dropped significantly and I couldn't keep up with assignments. The stress built up even though I wasn't feeling anxious exactly—more like defeated and sad about the whole situation.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was trek to campus just to use the internet. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did study. Competing responsibilities meant the course often lost out to more immediate needs like earning rent money. The LMS became something I dreaded rather than a helpful tool.
Q14: There were times I'd log in and just stare at the screen, clicking through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd watch videos but my mind would wander to bills or work schedules. I might technically complete a task but feel like nothing actually stuck. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through motions without real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was not having internet to submit work when I actually finished it at home. Part of it was this low motivation—knowing I was already behind made it hard to start. The CBT sessions helped me understand this pattern better, but breaking it was another matter entirely.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts where I'd rush to campus, try to download and complete everything at once, then disappear again. It wasn't healthy or effective. I'd often miss deadlines simply because I couldn't get to the internet in time. The pressure of deadlines without reliable access created a cycle of stress and incomplete work.
Q17: When I got feedback showing my assignment work was actually strong—like scoring 97 on assignments I did complete—it gave me a temporary boost. It showed me I could do the work when I actually engaged with it. But then missing the next deadline would bring me back down. The positive feedback felt distant from my daily struggle to just participate.
Q18: The course structure assumed consistent internet access and regular attendance, which just wasn't my reality. Weekly modules with tight deadlines didn't match my irregular schedule. I would have benefited from more flexibility—maybe longer windows for submissions or more downloadable content. The structure worked against students in situations like mine.
Q19: The video content being downloadable made engagement easier—that was a lifesaver. But the discussion requirements and timed activities were harder because they needed real-time participation I couldn't always manage. The exam being so heavily weighted also meant my poor attendance hurt my grade despite decent assignment work. More balance would have helped.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I logged in 45 times and watched videos, participated in discussions when I could, but my assignment completion was low because of access issues and timing. High activity might mean someone is trying hard but facing barriers to actually submitting work. The system shows effort but not the obstacles preventing completion.
Q21: That's interesting because my experience was sort of the opposite—low visible activity but when I did submit assignments, I scored well. I think students with good study skills and offline resources can learn effectively without constant LMS presence. The platform doesn't capture studying from downloaded materials or learning through other means.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login times and what resources get accessed most. They can flag students who disappear entirely. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, personal circumstances affecting participation, and whether someone is actually learning versus just clicking through. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just passing. It's thinking about concepts outside of class, connecting ideas to real life, wanting to discuss topics with others. For me, engagement happened in my headphones on the bus more than in the LMS. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my financial stress, my work schedule conflicts, or that I don't have internet at home. It wouldn't show me listening to the same lecture five times while doing dishes, or the sadness I felt watching my grade slip despite genuine effort. It wouldn't show that my assignment quality proved I could do the work when circumstances allowed. My story is more complicated than my login count suggests.
